messages.content:
### Lesson 1: Fractional-Order Calculus in Robotics
**Summary of Key Concepts**

Fractional-order calculus extends traditional integer-order calculus by enabling the differentiation and integration of non-integer orders. This mathematical framework provides a robust tool for modeling complex, non-linear dynamic systems where traditional methods often fail due to environmental uncertainties and internal system memory properties [S.10].

#### 1. Core Advantages in Robotics
*   **Memory and Hereditary Properties:** Fractional models capture the "memory" of a system, making them more resilient to the non-linearities and parameter variations common in robotic environments [S.10].
*   **Enhanced Control Performance:** Fractional-order controllers, such as the Fractional-Order Proportional-Integral-Derivative (FOPID) controller, significantly improve system stability, rapidity, and precision [S.10, S.11].
*   **Optimization:** The framework aids in more efficient motion planning, trajectory optimization, and reduced energy consumption for autonomous systems [S.10].

#### 2. Applied Research Areas
The provided document showcases a diverse range of practical implementations, including:
*   **Control Schemes:** Fractional active disturbance rejection for Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) and impedance control for robot manipulators [S.11, S.12].
*   **Path Planning:** Improving navigation for car-like mobile robots through enhanced Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) [S.11].
*   **Soft Robotics:** Utilizing fully fractional Generalized Maxwell models to describe the viscoelastic behavior of dielectric elastomer actuators [S.12].
*   **Advanced Forecasting:** Combining hybrid LSTM neural networks with fractional-order activation functions for renewable energy (wind power) prediction [S.11].
*   **System Integration:** Applying digital-twin frameworks to optimize the real-time performance of industrial robots [S.11].

#### 3. Strategic Significance
The adoption of fractional calculus constitutes a "significant leap forward" in the field, enabling the development of more autonomous and reliable robotic systems capable of navigating unpredictable, high-stakes environments [S.11, S.12]. The field is currently transitioning from theoretical exploration toward high-impact practical applications, such as precision agriculture and hazardous infrastructure maintenance [S.8, S.15].

Lesson 1: Fractional-Order Calculus in Robotics - Presentation10 slides
Fractional-Order Calculus in Robotics
  • Advanced mathematical approach for robotic control.
  • Handles nonlinear dynamics, uncertainties, and constraints (p. 15).

The provided document is a collection of research articles published as a Special Issue of the journal *Fractal and Fractional*, titled "Applications of Fractional-Order Calculus in Robotics," edited by Abhaya Pal Singh and Kishore Bingi [S.1, S.4].

### Core Concept of Fractional Calculus
Fractional calculus is a branch of mathematical analysis that generalizes derivatives and integrals to non-integer, arbitrary orders [S.10]. Dating back to the 17th century with the work of Leibniz, Newton, Riemann, and Liouville, it has evolved from a theoretical curiosity into a fundamental tool for modeling complex physical systems [S.10].

### Importance in Robotics
The integration of fractional-order calculus into robotics addresses the limitations of traditional integer-order models when dealing with system memory, hereditary properties, and environmental uncertainties [S.10]. Key advantages include:
* Enhanced Control Systems: Tools such as fractional-order proportional integral derivative (FOPID) controllers offer superior management of system non-linearities and parameter variations [S.10].
* Improved Performance: Researchers utilize these models to achieve more precise trajectory optimization and path planning, resulting in smoother robotic movement and reduced energy consumption [S.10].
* Versatile Applications: The collection covers a wide range of practical implementations, including robotic manipulators, pneumatic actuators, wind farm power forecasting, optimization for industrial robots, and positioning control for remotely operated vehicles [S.6].

### Editorial Background
The editors, Abhaya Pal Singh (Norwegian University of Life Sciences) and Kishore Bingi (Universiti Teknologi PETRONAS), are experts in control systems, robotics, and fractional-order neural networks. Their work in this compilation aims to bridge the gap between advanced mathematical theory and practical, autonomous robotic engineering [S.8].

This document provides a comprehensive review of the application of fractional-order calculus in the modeling and control of robotic manipulators. The following summarizes the key findings from the provided text:

**Technological Motivation**
Robotic manipulators are highly coupled, non-linear systems often subjected to time-varying constraints and uncertainties. Because these factors negatively impact system performance, engineers increasingly utilize fractional-order concepts to develop more robust mathematical models and adaptive control strategies.

**Methodological Preliminaries**
The fractional-order differintegral operator is defined as:
$$D^\alpha_t f(t) = \begin{cases} \frac{d^\alpha}{dt^\alpha} f(t), & \alpha > 0 \\ f(t), & \alpha = 0 \\ \int^t_0 f(\tau) d\tau, & \alpha < 0 \end{cases}$$
The most commonly referenced definitions for fractional derivatives are the Grünwald–Letnikov, Riemann–Liouville, and Caputo methods. For practical implementation, approximation techniques—most notably Oustaloup’s method—are employed to estimate these fractional orders within specific frequency ranges ([S.15]-[S.17]).

**Research Trends and Observations**
Based on the survey of 95 works summarized in Table 1:
- The majority of research focuses on robotic manipulators with 1, 2, or 3 degrees of freedom (DOF), with 2-DOF systems representing approximately 60% of studies ([S.17]).
- Approximately 66% of existing research is conducted on manipulators without payloads, indicating a significant area for future development ([S.17]).
- Research spans various manipulator types, including SCARA, PUMA 560, and several flexible-link systems ([S.17]).
- Common controller strategies mentioned include fractional-order PID (proportion-integral-derivative), sliding mode, fuzzy logic, backstepping, and active disturbance rejection control. These are often optimized using techniques like Genetic Algorithms, Particle Swarm Optimization, and Cuckoo Search Algorithms ([S.18]-[S.21]).

**Future Directions**
The review highlights the shift toward using fractional calculus to solve complex, robust control challenges. Future research is expected to continue addressing the performance gaps in higher DOF systems and the handling of variable payload conditions to improve precision and efficiency in manufacturing and hazardous-environment applications ([S.15]-[S.16]).

This paper introduces a Fractional-Order (FO) impedance control strategy for robot manipulators to address the limitations of traditional integer-order viscoelastic damping models.

Key Points:

The Limitation of Traditional Models: Conventional impedance control relies on a linear mass-spring-damper model that often fails to accurately describe the dynamic behavior of robot-environment interactions. This results in suboptimal force tracking, overshoot, and reduced robustness in uncertain environments.

Proposed Fractional-Order Controller: The authors propose using fractional calculus to model the damping force, which provides greater flexibility and accuracy compared to the integer-order model. The dynamic equation is represented as:
$M_d \ddot{x}(t) + B_d \dot{x}^\mu (t) + K_d x(t) = F_{ext}$
where $\mu$ is the fractional order. 

Controller Design and Tuning: A systematic frequency-domain design method is developed to determine controller parameters ($M_d$, $B_d$, and $\mu$). The process involves:
1. Setting specifications for gain crossover frequency and phase margin to ensure a fair comparison between controllers.
2. Eliminating the stiffness parameter ($K_d = 0$) to reduce steady-state force-tracking errors.
3. Using an optimization approach where the fractional order $\mu$ is swept and the Integral Time Square Error (ITSE) is calculated to select the optimal parameters.

Performance Verification: Simulation and experimental tests comparing the FO-impedance controller against an optimized integer-order (IO) controller demonstrate that the FO-impedance controller achieves:
- Improved step response performance, characterized by smaller overshoot and reduced settling time.
- Better anti-disturbance capabilities and lower ITSE values.
- Faster recovery time and higher stability during interactions. 

The authors conclude that the proposed FO-impedance framework effectively enhances the compliance control of robot manipulators in complex or uncertain working conditions.

This research investigates the viscoelastic behavior of dielectric elastomer actuators (DEAs), particularly their time-dependent sinusoidal creep during initial cycles of dynamic excitation. The study introduces a fully fractional generalised Maxwell model to capture these effects, offering a simpler alternative to complex continuum mechanics approaches.

Key points include:

Modeling Approach
The authors derive the model using the Laplace transform, which converts fractional derivatives into practical mathematical operations for control simulation. Each branch of the Maxwell model is represented by "springpot" elements, which replace traditional dashpots to account for viscoelastic properties through fractional derivative orders $\alpha$ ($0 < \alpha < 1$). The final transfer function is expressed as:

$$ \frac{x(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1}{ms^2 + c_{1,1}s^{\alpha_{1,1}} + \sum_{i=2}^{n+1} (\frac{1}{c_{i,1}s^{\alpha_{i,1}}} + \frac{1}{c_{i,2}s^{\alpha_{i,2}}})^{-1}} $$

Experimental Setup
Experiments were conducted using VHB 4910 elastomer strips subjected to sinusoidal voltage excitations of 6 kV across three distinct frequencies ($1/13$ Hz, $1/7$ Hz, and $1/5$ Hz). Displacement was captured via laser sensors to provide data for model validation.

Optimization and Results
The parameter identification process utilized the "Pattern Search" global optimization algorithm. The study evaluated model performance using the coefficient of determination ($R^2$) by comparing experimental data with simulation results generated via the Matlab function $lsim$. 

Conclusion
The derived model successfully describes the viscoelastic creep of DEAs while remaining computationally efficient for control simulation implementation. The method is versatile and can be applied to various viscoelastic materials, minimizing the complexity associated with internal variables and governing equations found in conventional modeling techniques.

This paper presents two control design methodologies for a two-axis positioning mechatronic system, focusing on improving performance and handling parametric uncertainties.

### Key Contributions
The authors address the limitations of previous studies by:
* Integrating parametric uncertainties into an LMI-based control framework to guarantee D-stability for an uncertain model.
* Converting Polytopic LDI (PLDI) models into Diagonal Norm-bound LDI (DNLDI) systems to reduce computational complexity and manage command signal saturation.
* Developing a cascade control structure (PI position loop, P velocity loop) derived from optimal state-feedback gains.
* Proposing an autotuning procedure for a fractional-order $I^\lambda D^\mu$ controller by using relay-induced limit cycles to determine parameters such as gain-crossover frequency and phase margins.

### Methodology
1. **LMI-Based Design**: The system is modeled using linear differential inclusions. By defining D-regions (specifically vertical strips for settling time and conic sectors for overshoot), the authors solve LMIs such that the closed-loop system is stable and meets performance criteria. The state-feedback gain $K$ is derived from $K = ZP^{-1}$.
2. **Cascade Transformation**: The plant model is augmented with integral states $z_x$ and $z_y$ to enable PI-based tracking for the position and velocity loops.
3. **Fractional-Order Design**: This approach uses a secondary controller configuration that generalizes classical PID control by adding extra degrees of freedom, providing increased robustness for the mechatronic system.

### Scope
The study applies these design procedures to a two-axis CNC machine. The performance of the two proposed control structures is evaluated and compared against quantifiable metrics including rise time, settling time, overshoot, and overall robustness.

This study proposes a five-dimensional digital twin framework designed to enhance the real-time monitoring and performance optimization of industrial robots. The framework addresses the need for smarter cyber-physical manufacturing by bridging the gap between virtual models and physical systems (p. 90, 92).

### Key Components of the Framework
The framework consists of five dimensions: the physical system, the virtual system, digital twin data, service applications, and the connectivity between them (p. 92). Implementation is structured into four distinct phases:

1. Multi-Domain Modeling: Establishes a 3D representation that incorporates geometric, kinematic, and dynamic characteristics to enable real-time visual monitoring and motion tracking (p. 93-94, 95).
2. Behavioral Matching: Ensures the virtual model accurately reflects the current state of the physical system through an iterative optimization process, minimizing the difference between physical and virtual outputs (p. 94).
3. Control Optimization: Integrates Fractional Order Control (FOC) to improve system dynamics. By utilizing artificial intelligence algorithms for parameter tuning, the framework overcomes the inefficiencies of manual, offline adjustments (p. 91, 94-95).
4. Parameter Updating: Transfers optimized controller parameters from the virtual environment to the physical system in real time, maintaining high performance during operational changes (p. 95).

### Technical Innovations and Results
The research focuses on the deployment of fractional order controllers, specifically the $PI^{\lambda}D^{\mu}$ controller, which offers greater flexibility and tracking performance than traditional integer-order controllers (p. 91). 

By leveraging an improved particle swarm optimization algorithm within this digital twin framework, the authors achieved significant performance gains in experimental testing:
* Overshoot improved by at least 42%.
* Peak time improved by at least 32%.
* Settling time improved by at least 33% (p. 90).

The authors conclude that this approach successfully resolves challenges related to real-time behavioral matching and the automated optimization of physical control systems, demonstrating clear effectiveness for industrial motion applications (p. 91, 95).

This study explores the implementation of a Fractional Order Proportional Tilt Integral Derivative (FOPTID) controller to enhance the trajectory tracking performance of a 3-DOF serial robotic manipulator.

### Key Objectives and Motivation
Robotic manipulators are non-linear, coupled systems prone to internal uncertainties and external disturbances. Traditional PID controllers often fail to provide the high-precision tracking required in advanced industrial, medical, and space applications. This research introduces a hybrid FOPTID controller to improve robustness and tracking accuracy.

### Proposed Controller Design
*   **FOPTID Controller:** Combines the features of a Tilt-Integral-Derivative (TID) controller with Fractional Order (FO) calculus. The inclusion of non-integer derivative and integral orders $(\alpha, \beta)$ provides extra flexibility for tuning.
*   **TID Advantage:** By replacing the proportional term of a PID with a tilt term ($s^{-1/n}$), the TID controller achieves superior disturbance rejection and reduces sensitivity to system parameter variations.
*   **Optimization:** The controller parameters are tuned using a hybrid Gray Wolf Optimizer and Particle Swarm Optimization (GWO–PSO) algorithm, which optimizes performance based on time integral criteria.

### Main Contributions and Results
*   **Superior Accuracy:** Comparisons with PID, FOPID, and PTID controllers demonstrate that the proposed FOPTID model reduces joint tracking errors by approximately 20.2% to 44.5%.
*   **Energy Efficiency:** The total energy consumption of the manipulator is reduced by 2.45% under the proposed control strategy.
*   **Robustness:** The FOPTID controller demonstrates exceptional stability against external disturbances, joint friction, and varying trajectory requirements.

### Mathematical Framework
The rigid body dynamics of the 3-DOF robot are defined by the equation:
$$\tau = D(\theta)\ddot{\theta} + C(\theta, \dot{\theta}) + G(\theta) + \tau_f$$
where $D(\theta)$ is the inertia matrix, $C(\theta, \dot{\theta})$ is the Coriolis/centripetal matrix, $G(\theta)$ represents gravity, and $\tau_f$ accounts for friction and disturbances. Fractional operators are implemented via the definition:
$$_a D_t^\alpha f(t)$$
This allows for non-integer orders of integration and differentiation, providing the system with a greater degree of control freedom compared to traditional integer-order methods.

This research explores the design and implementation of a Fuzzy Fractional Order PID (Fuzzy FOPID) controller to improve the positioning performance of a Pneumatic Actuated Ball and Beam (PABB) system.

**Key Components and Methodology:**

**System Modeling**
*   **Intelligent Pneumatic Actuator (IPA):** The researchers used a system identification (SI) technique to derive a third-order linear ARX model (p. 129). This approach addresses the inherent non-linearities of the pneumatic system, such as air compressibility and friction.
*   **Ball and Beam (PABB):** The system dynamics were derived using the Lagrangian method. The resulting transfer function relates the pneumatic actuator stroke ($h$) to the ball position ($x$) as follows (p. 132):
$$\frac{X(s)}{H(s)} = \frac{-0.115mg}{(J_b/r^2 + m)s^2}$$

**Control Strategy**
*   **Fuzzy FOPID vs. Fuzzy PID:** The study employs a dual-loop control architecture. An inner loop controls the pneumatic actuator's stroke, while an outer loop regulates the ball's position. The proposed Fuzzy FOPID controller integrates fuzzy logic with fractional-order control to enhance precision, stability, and robustness compared to traditional PID methods (p. 130).
*   **Optimization:** A Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm was utilized to tune the seven parameters of the Fuzzy FOPID controller to achieve optimal dynamic performance (p. 130).

**Experimental Validation**
*   **Performance:** The proposed system was validated through both simulations and real-time experiments. The results indicate that the intelligent pneumatic actuator, when integrated with the Fuzzy FOPID controller, provides superior rapidity, stability, and precision in controlling the PABB system (p. 128).

This paper proposes a hybrid forecasting model for wind power integration, specifically addressing the challenges of variable wind patterns and missing sensor data on Jeju Island. 

Key findings and methodology:

1. Data Challenges: Wind power prediction is often hindered by missing data due to sensor malfunctions or communication issues. Accurate forecasting of wind speed and direction is necessary to maintain power grid stability.

2. Hybrid Model Framework: The researchers developed a two-stage approach:
- Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks: Used to predict and fill in missing wind speed and direction data, ensuring a reliable dataset for further analysis (p. 159).
- Fractional-Order Neural Network (FONN): This model incorporates a fractional arctan activation function, which allows for the representation of complex non-linear relationships and fractal-like behavior inherent in wind energy data (p. 161).

3. Performance and Evaluation:
- The methodology was tested across three sites on Jeju Island with varying turbine specifications (p. 162). 
- Correlation analysis confirmed a strong relationship between wind speed and wind power across all sites, particularly at Site C (p. 164).
- The hybrid system improves forecasting accuracy, with performance validated through metrics such as Mean Squared Error (MSE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and the coefficient of determination $R^2$ (p. 162).

4. Conclusion: The integration of LSTMs for data completion and FONNs for power output prediction addresses the limitations of conventional neural networks, offering a robust solution for renewable energy management even when input data is incomplete (p. 161, 162).

To address the challenges of path planning for car-like mobile robots (CLMRs) in complex environments, this research presents an improved path planning framework based on fractional-order modeling and Ant Colony Optimization (ACO). (p. 184)

The study identifies key limitations in traditional integer-order kinematic models when applied to CLMRs equipped with suspension systems. Since these systems possess time-varying steering constraints, the authors propose a novel fractional-order-based kinematic modeling approach. This method utilizes the Grünwald-Letnikov definition:

$$D^{\alpha} [f(t)] = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{h^{\alpha}} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (-1)^n \binom{\alpha}{n} f(t-nh)$$

(p. 188)

The research further introduces an enhanced ACO algorithm to generate efficient and smooth paths. Key improvements include:

- Global multifactorial heuristic functions that improve search speed.
- Dynamic angle constraints integrated from the fractional-order kinematic model to better handle narrow spaces.
- Adaptive pheromone adjustment rules to prevent the algorithm from falling into local optima.
- Fractional-order descriptive state-transfer models to ensure path smoothness. (p. 186-187)

The proposed framework enables CLMRs to navigate narrow and large-scale scenarios more effectively than traditional methods. The effectiveness of this approach was validated through experiments, confirming that the combination of fractional-order modeling and improved ACO enhances both the success rate and the smoothness of planned paths. (p. 186-187)
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Why is fractional-order calculus considered more advantageous than traditional integer-order models for complex robotic systems?
options: It allows for the representation of systems with memory and hereditary properties.
It simplifies robotic control by ignoring non-linearities and parameter variations.
It requires significantly less computational power than integer-order calculus.
It eliminates the need for any mathematical modeling of robotic systems.
questions.explanation: The text notes that fractional calculus offers a refined approach because of its capacity to model systems with memory and hereditary properties, which integer-order models often fail to capture.

questions.question: Based on the provided editorial, what is a primary practical benefit of using fractional-order controllers in robotics?
options: They enable the robot to perform tasks perfectly even if the sensors are broken.
They ensure the robot will never experience physical mechanical wear.
They eliminate the need for any trajectory planning, as the robot becomes fully autonomous.
They enhance performance and reliability when managing non-linearities and parameter variations.
questions.explanation: Fractional-order controllers, such as FOPID, excel at managing system non-linearities and parameter variations, leading to improved performance, reliability, and smoother operations.

questions.question: How did the approach to path planning mentioned in the article by Likun Li et al. improve mobile robot navigation?
options: By forcing robots to move at fixed speeds regardless of the environment.
By using fractional-order kinematics and an improved ant colony optimization with dynamic constraints.
By replacing all mechanical suspension systems with purely mathematical software algorithms.
By removing the need for pheromone-based adjustments in path calculation.
questions.explanation: The article uses an accurate fractional-order-based kinematic modeling method combined with an improved ACO-based path planning method that includes dynamic angle constraints and adaptive pheromone adjustment.

questions.question: What role does the digital-twin-based framework described by Xuan Liu et al. play in industrial robotics?
options: It facilitates real-time monitoring and control optimization to improve time-domain performance.
It is used exclusively for training human operators on how to use robotic hardware.
It serves only as a visual representation without impacting actual robot control.
It replaces the physical robot entirely with a software-only simulation.
questions.explanation: The framework incorporates multi-domain modeling and control optimization, which experimental validation showed leads to substantial enhancements in time-domain performance, such as reduced overshoot and settling time.

questions.question: What is the primary motivation for using a fractional-order approach for dielectric elastomer actuators, according to Timi Karner et al.?
options: To decrease the number of branches needed in a mathematical model to zero.
To force the actuator to behave like a rigid, non-deformable mechanical structure.
To accurately model the viscoelastic behavior of the material using a generalized Maxwell model.
To eliminate the need for electrical energy in soft robotic systems.
questions.explanation: Dielectric elastomer actuators exhibit viscoelastic behavior; the authors derived a fully fractional generalized Maxwell model to capture this specific behavior.

cards.front: What characterizes fractional calculus in comparison to traditional (integer-order) calculus?
cards.back: Fractional calculus allows for the differentiation and integration of non-integer, arbitrary orders, providing a more robust framework for modeling systems with memory and hereditary properties.

cards.front: Why is fractional calculus particularly advantageous for robotic control systems?
cards.back: It excels at modeling dynamic systems, managing system non-linearities, and maintaining performance under unforeseeable disturbances and fluctuating load conditions that traditional integer-order models may struggle with.

cards.front: What is the primary benefit of a Fractional-Order PID (FOPID) controller?
cards.back: FOPID controllers provide enhanced performance, reliability, and robustness compared to integer-order PID controllers by effectively managing parameter variations and non-linear system behaviors.

cards.front: How does fractional calculus influence robotic path planning and trajectory optimization?
cards.back: Integrating fractional-order models allows for algorithms that optimize paths more effectively, ensuring smoother transitions, higher maneuvering accuracy, and reduced energy consumption.

cards.front: What does the 'fractional active disturbance rejection' control scheme for ROVs achieve?
cards.back: It enables high-precision tasks by effectively resisting disturbances and maintaining performance without requiring perfectly accurate model data.

cards.front: What role does fractional-order calculus play in the context of soft robotics and dielectric elastomer actuators?
cards.back: It is used to describe the complex viscoelastic behavior of these actuators by deriving fractional-order generalized Maxwell models.

cards.front: How is fractional calculus applied to improve wind power forecasting?
cards.back: It is used within fractional-order neural networks (often employing fractional activation functions) to enhance the accuracy of wind power prediction and address missing data gaps.

cards.front: What is a 'Digital Twin' in the context of industrial robotics research presented in this issue?
cards.back: It is a framework for real-time monitoring and performance optimization that incorporates multi-domain modeling, behavioral matching, and fractional-order controllers to improve metrics like overshoot and settling time.

cards.front: What is the primary function of a Fractional-Order Impedance Controller for robot manipulators?
cards.back: It utilizes fractional calculus to describe damping forces more accurately than traditional models, leading to superior step response and anti-disturbance performance.

cards.front: According to the editorial, what is a key focus of the review article provided by Bingi et al.?
cards.back: The review covers state-of-the-art fractional-order modeling and control strategies for robotic manipulators, summarizing approximately 95 related works to provide an updated knowledge base for the control engineering community.

slides.title: Fractional-Order Calculus in Robotics
bullets: Advanced mathematical approach for robotic control.
Handles nonlinear dynamics, uncertainties, and constraints (p. 15).
slides.notes: Fractional-order calculus extends traditional control by providing robust solutions for complex, coupled, and non-linear robotic systems.

slides.title: Modelling Robotic Manipulators
bullets: Manipulators are non-linear, highly coupled systems (p. 15).
Fractional calculus offers superior modeling accuracy.
Addresses time-varying constraints and system uncertainties (p. 15).
Provides robust alternatives to integer-order techniques (p. 16).
slides.notes: Engineers face challenges managing uncertainties in manipulators; fractional-order approaches provide effective modelling and control strategies.

slides.title: Fractional-Order Impedance Control
bullets: Improves robot-environment dynamic interaction (p. 44).
Damping force modeled accurately using fractional calculus.
Reduces overshoot and settling time in responses.
Enhances stability and disturbance rejection capabilities (p. 44).
slides.notes: By replacing traditional linear viscoelastic models with fractional-order descriptions, we achieve better precision in contact force management.

slides.title: Viscoelastic Actuator Modelling
bullets: Applies fully fractional generalized Maxwell models (p. 59).
Captures viscoelastic effects in dielectric elastomer actuators.
Used Laplace transform for constitutive model derivation.
Optimization via Pattern Search improves implementation (p. 59).
slides.notes: The unique viscoelastic behaviour of flexible actuators is efficiently captured through these fractional-order models, facilitating better control simulations.

slides.title: Cascade Control for Mechatronics
bullets: Designed for two-axis position mechatronic systems (p. 73).
Utilizes LMI-based robust optimization techniques.
Employs fractional-order PIλIDμ controllers.
Balances tracking performance with command constraints (p. 73).
slides.notes: This approach compares optimization-based LMI techniques with fractional-order PID control to improve accuracy in two-axis positioning systems.

slides.title: Digital Twin Optimization
bullets: Integrates digital twins for real-time robotic monitoring.
Uses fractional-order control (FOC) for optimization (p. 90).
Improves overshoot and settling time significantly.
Validates performance via multi-domain simulation models (p. 90).
slides.notes: Digital twins bridge virtual and physical systems, where FOC optimized by particle swarm algorithms boosts industrial robot efficiency.

slides.title: Robust Trajectory Tracking
bullets: Implements fractional-order PTID controller for 3-DOF robots (p. 106).
Hybrid GWO-PSO optimization tunes controller parameters.
Reduces trajectory tracking errors and energy consumption.
Maintains stability under external disturbances (p. 106).
slides.notes: The proposed FOPTID controller offers superior trajectory following and robustness compared to standard PID or FOPID strategies.

slides.title: Fuzzy Fractional-Order Control
bullets: Applied to pneumatic ball-beam (PABB) systems (p. 128).
Optimizes Fuzzy FOPID controller using PSO algorithm.
System identification generates accurate pneumatic models (p. 128).
Achieves superior rapidity and precision in positioning.
slides.notes: By combining fuzzy logic with fractional-order PID, we effectively mitigate the complex, non-linear behaviour inherent in pneumatic systems.

slides.title: Wind Farm Power Forecasting
bullets: Hybrid LSTM and fractional-order neural networks (p. 159).
Addresses data gaps in wind speed and direction.
Fractional arctan activation function enhances prediction.
Increases forecast accuracy for grid integration (p. 159).
slides.notes: This hybrid AI-fractional calculus approach handles the non-linear, variable nature of wind energy to optimize power grid operations.

slides.title: Path Planning for Mobile Robots
bullets: Fractional-order kinematics for mobile car-like robots (p. 184).
Improved Ant Colony Optimization for narrow spaces.
Ensures smooth, efficient paths in large-scale scenes.
Dynamic angle constraints prevent local optima (p. 184).
slides.notes: By using fractional-order modelling, path planning in complex environments becomes more precise, allowing robots to navigate tight spaces effectively.
